title: Quiz: Digital Signatures
description: Test your understanding of digital signatures, RSA-PSS, ECDSA, and quantum vulnerabilities

questions.question: Which three security properties do digital signatures provide?
questions.explanation: Digital signatures provide Authentication (proving who signed), Integrity (detecting modifications), and Non-repudiation (signer cannot deny signing). Note: signatures do NOT provide confidentiality—the message is not encrypted.

questions.question: Why do we hash a message before signing it?

options.text: Hashing encrypts the message for confidentiality
Signing a fixed-size hash is efficient and provides security benefits
The signature algorithm requires the input to be exactly 256 bits
Hashing is required by law for digital signatures

questions.explanation: Hash-then-sign is efficient (signing a small fixed-size hash regardless of message size) and secure (prevents certain attacks that require signing chosen data). The signature scheme then operates on the hash.

questions.question: What is the main advantage of Ed25519 over ECDSA?

options.text: Ed25519 is deterministic, avoiding randomness failures
Ed25519 uses larger key sizes for more security

questions.explanation: Ed25519 is deterministic—it derives the random value from the message and private key instead of requiring a random number generator. This eliminates the catastrophic vulnerability where ECDSA with a reused or predictable random value completely breaks security.

questions.question: What happens if the same random value 'k' is used twice in ECDSA?

options.text: The private key can be recovered from the two signatures

questions.explanation: Reusing k in ECDSA is catastrophic! Given two signatures with the same k, an attacker can solve for the private key using simple algebra. This has led to real-world attacks including the PlayStation 3 hack and Bitcoin theft.

questions.question: RSA-PSS is preferred over PKCS#1 v1.5 for new signature implementations.
questions.explanation: True. RSA-PSS (Probabilistic Signature Scheme) is randomized and has provable security properties. PKCS#1 v1.5 is deterministic and has known vulnerabilities. Always use RSA-PSS for new applications.

questions.question: Approximately how large is an ECDSA P-256 signature compared to an RSA-2048 signature?

options.text: ECDSA is about 4x smaller (64 bytes vs 256 bytes)

questions.explanation: ECDSA P-256 signatures are approximately 64 bytes, while RSA-2048 signatures are 256 bytes—about 4x smaller. This makes ECC signatures much more efficient for bandwidth-constrained applications.

questions.question: Which statement about quantum computers and digital signatures is TRUE?

options.text: Only RSA signatures are vulnerable; ECDSA is quantum-safe
Digital signatures are not affected by quantum computers
All currently deployed signature schemes (RSA, ECDSA, Ed25519) are broken by Shor's algorithm
Quantum computers can only break signatures, not create forgeries

questions.explanation: Shor's algorithm breaks all signature schemes based on factoring (RSA) or discrete logarithms (DSA, ECDSA, Ed25519). A quantum attacker could forge signatures for any public key, impersonating anyone.

questions.question: Which of the following are valid use cases for digital signatures? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Code signing to verify software authenticity

questions.explanation: Digital signatures are used for TLS certificates (authentication), code signing (software verification), and cryptocurrency transactions (proving ownership). Encryption is NOT a signature use case—signatures provide authentication, not confidentiality.
